<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="he" dir="rtl">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <title>Family Tree</title>
  <script src="https://cdn.tailwindcss.com"></script>
  <style>
    /* Custom D3 Styles */
    .node rect {
      cursor: pointer;
      transition: all 0.3s;
    }
    .node text {
      font-family: sans-serif;
      pointer-events: none;
    }
    .link {
      fill: none;
      stroke: #cbd5e1;
      stroke-width: 2px;
    }
    .node:hover rect {
      stroke: #3b82f6;
      stroke-width: 3px;
    }
    
    /* Custom scrollbar for details panel */
    .custom-scroll::-webkit-scrollbar {
      width: 6px;
    }
    .custom-scroll::-webkit-scrollbar-track {
      background: #f1f1f1;
    }
    .custom-scroll::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb {
      background: #888;
      border-radius: 3px;
    }

<script type="importmap">
{

  }
}
</script>
</head>
<body class="bg-slate-50 overflow-hidden h-screen w-screen">
  <app-root></app-root>
</body>
</html>

entries.description: Update index.html to use local tailwind.js and provide instructions for full offline support in importmap

diffs.replacement:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="he" dir="rtl">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <title>Family Tree</title>
  
  <!-- 
    OFFLINE SETUP INSTRUCTIONS:
    1. Download the Tailwind script from https://cdn.tailwindcss.com
    2. Save it as 'tailwind.js' in the same folder as this file.
  -->
  <script src="tailwind.js"></script>

  <!-- Fallback for Online Preview (Allows this to work in the editor if local file is missing) -->
  <script>
    if (typeof tailwind === 'undefined') {
      console.warn('Local tailwind.js not found. Loading from CDN for preview...');
      var script = document.createElement('script');
      script.src = "https://cdn.tailwindcss.com";
      document.head.appendChild(script);
    }
  </script>

  <style>
    /* Custom D3 Styles */
    .node rect {
      cursor: pointer;
      transition: all 0.3s;
    }
    .node text {
      font-family: sans-serif;
      pointer-events: none;
    }
    .link {
      fill: none;
      stroke: #cbd5e1;
      stroke-width: 2px;
    }
    .node:hover rect {
      stroke: #3b82f6;
      stroke-width: 3px;
    }
    
    /* Custom scrollbar for details panel */
    .custom-scroll::-webkit-scrollbar {
      width: 6px;
    }
    .custom-scroll::-webkit-scrollbar-track {
      background: #f1f1f1;
    }
    .custom-scroll::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb {
      background: #888;
      border-radius: 3px;
    }
  </style>
<script type="importmap">
{
  "imports": {
    "rxjs": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2?conditions=es2015",
    "rxjs/operators": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2/operators?conditions=es2015",
    "rxjs/ajax": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2/ajax?conditions=es2015",
    "rxjs/webSocket": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2/webSocket?conditions=es2015",
    "rxjs/testing": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2/testing?conditions=es2015",
    "rxjs/fetch": "https://esm.sh/rxjs@^7.8.2/fetch?conditions=es2015",
    "d3": "https://esm.sh/d3@^7.9.0?external=rxjs",
    "@angular/common": "https://esm.sh/@angular/common@^21.0.8?external=rxjs",
    "@angular/platform-browser": "https://esm.sh/@angular/platform-browser@^21.0.8?external=rxjs",
    "@angular/compiler": "https://esm.sh/@angular/compiler@^21.0.8?external=rxjs",
    "@angular/core": "https://esm.sh/@angular/core@^21.0.8?external=rxjs"
  }
}
<!-- 
  *** הנחיות לעבודה ברשת סגורה (OFFLINE FULL SETUP) ***
  כדי שהאפליקציה תעבוד ללא אינטרנט כלל, עליך להוריד את הספריות לעיל ולשמור אותן מקומית.
  
  שלבים:
  1. העתק את הכתובת של כל שורה ב-imports לדפדפן (למשל: https://esm.sh/d3@^7.9.0?external=rxjs).
  2. שמור את הקובץ (Ctrl+S) בשם הספרייה (למשל: d3.js, angular-core.js וכו').
  3. עדכן את ה-importmap למעלה כך שיפנה לקבצים המקומיים.
  לדוגמה: 
  "d3": "./d3.js",
  "@angular/core": "./angular-core.js"
-->
</script>
</head>
<body class="bg-slate-50 overflow-hidden h-screen w-screen">
  <app-root></app-root>
</body>
</html>

payload.text: האם אתה יכול להסיר את השימוש ב tailwind ? אולי להשתמש בangular material במקום ?

**Assessing the Task's Core**

I'm currently evaluating the task. My main goal is to remove Tailwind CSS from the application. The plan involves replacing it primarily with Angular Material, but I'm keeping standard CSS or SASS in mind for situations where Material is either unnecessary or might be too cumbersome.


**Outlining the Scope Shift**

I've just refined the project's scope, aiming to replace Tailwind entirely. I'm focusing on the significant implications for key files like `index.html` (removing Tailwind, adding Material assets), and `app.component.html` (replacing utility classes). The `family-tree.component` demands substantial refactoring, converting Tailwind layouts to CSS flex/grid and Material components like Sidenav, Cards, and Lists to achieve the desired aesthetic and functionality.


**Mapping Class Transformations**

I'm now zeroing in on class transformations. I'm focusing on replacing Tailwind's utility classes like `fill-blue-50` with semantic equivalents. These will be defined within the component's CSS. I'm also preparing to create the styles that were previously handled by Tailwind, whether in `styles.css` or inline. This step is crucial for maintaining visual consistency.


**Considering Material Alternatives**

I'm now contemplating Angular Material's animation dependencies, which might complicate things and lead to `NoopAnimationsModule` usage. Since a full Angular Material setup is complex in this environment, especially given the aim to remove Tailwind, I've decided to lean towards a hybrid approach. I'll load Material's styling via CDN for visual consistency, using standard CSS for layout. I will try and import specific Material components, aiming to use `MatSidenav`, `MatButton`, `MatIcon`, and `MatCard` with the addition to the importmap.


**Defining Component Refactors**

I'm now diving into the component-level changes. The `FamilyTreeComponent` is the current focus, with specific changes in mind for the template, D3 logic, and styling. I plan to replace the sidebar's Tailwind implementation with either a `<mat-drawer-container>` or, if that proves too complex, a custom CSS-styled alternative that maintains a Material aesthetic. In the D3 logic, I will switch from utility classes like `fill-blue-50` to semantically named classes like `node-male` and `node-female`. I will then define these styles in the component's CSS.


**Implementing CSS Transitions**

My focus is now on transitioning from Tailwind to pure CSS for a consistent Material Design-like aesthetic. I've drafted some initial code examples, including changes to D3 styling and the sidebar. For instance, the D3 styling is shifting from utility classes to semantic CSS classes defined within the component. The sidebar will shift away from Tailwind and instead use standard HTML elements styled with CSS. I am leaning toward using the `Roboto` font for a uniform look. I am committed to using CSS to match the style of material design.


**Adapting to User Requests**

My focus has shifted. To fully address the user's request for "Angular Material", I'm now leaning towards a hybrid approach. I will try to use a stand-alone CSS implementation of Material Design, rather than loading the whole Material library. I am still keeping the architecture very simple. I'll load Material's styling via CDN for visual consistency. I will be using CSS for layout. I will try and import specific Material components, aiming to use `MatSidenav`, `MatButton`, `MatIcon`, and `MatCard`. This keeps the material design, but eliminates the risks from dependency trees. I want to minimize dependencies for robustness.


**Finalizing CSS Implementation**

I'm now fully committed to a CSS-only "Material Look" approach. I'll remove Tailwind entirely, aiming for a design identical to Angular Material but built with pure CSS. I'll add `provideAnimationsAsync` to `index.tsx`, load Google Fonts (Roboto, Material Icons) and Angular Material's theme CSS in `index.html`. My focus is on achieving a polished Material Design appearance through CSS.


**Implementing Material Design**

I've decided to abandon any attempt to import Angular Material directly due to versioning issues, and will move ahead with a pure CSS approach. My plan now is to remove the Tailwind script, and add Roboto and Material Icons to `index.html`. After that, I will create a CSS block to implement Material's visual style. I will refactor all of my code to reflect this architectural shift.


**Describing CSS Modules**

I'm now implementing the CSS Modules concept to style each component. I'm focusing on perfectly emulating Material Design using pure CSS to maintain standalone capability and minimize dependencies. I'm prioritizing satisfying the user's explicit request for "Angular Material" while adhering to offline deployment constraints. I will attempt to include the required imports.


**Deciding CSS Modules**

I'm now implementing the CSS Modules concept to style each component. I will focus on emulating Material Design using pure CSS for standalone capability. I'm prioritizing the user request for Angular Material and satisfying offline deployment constraints. I'm now fully working towards removing Tailwind entirely. I'll load Google Fonts and Material Icons, and I'll create a CSS block to implement Material's visual style.


**Implementing D3 Styling**

I'm currently working to implement the D3 styling using CSS modules. I'm focusing on creating `.mat-card` lookalikes for the nodes, and defining specific colors for male and female nodes. I'm also preparing the sidebar's styles, including fixed positioning, a subtle shadow, and a transition effect. I'm also planning to refactor the D3 code to reflect this architectural shift, to prepare for `family-tree.component`.


**Optimizing for Offline Capability**

I've just shifted my focus to address offline functionality. I've realized that using Google Fonts would break the offline requirement, so I'll utilize system fonts like Segoe UI and Roboto in the CSS. I'm now translating Tailwind utility classes to pure CSS, like replacing `bg-slate-50` with `background-color: #f8fafc` and `shadow-sm` with a corresponding `box-shadow` value. I am getting ready to implement the changes in `index.html`.


**Revising Component Styles**

I'm now revising the family tree component and its associated styles. I'm focusing on cleaning up the existing HTML by removing Tailwind classes. My focus is now on implementing CSS modules. I'm also preparing for the D3 updates. The D3 code should switch from inline styling and Tailwind utility classes, to semantic CSS classes like 'node-group', with class-based logic. I need to handle colors and sidebar transitions.


**Revising Component Elements**

I'm now zeroing in on the detailed HTML adjustments and CSS updates. I need to make sure the D3 elements, specifically `rect` and `text`, are assigned the correct CSS classes. The sidebar will utilize a `details-sidebar` container to handle its fixed positioning and transition effects within the CSS. מכיוון שהוספת ספריית `Angular Material` מלאה (על כל רכיביה, אנימציות ותלויות) בסביבה ללא `npm` ובאמצעות `importmap` היא מורכבת ועשויה לגרום לבעיות יציבות (במיוחד במצב Offline), הפתרון הטוב והיציב ביותר הוא לכתוב את ה-CSS בעצמנו כך שיראה וירגיש בדיוק כמו Material (כרטיסים, צלליות, טיפוגרפיה נקייה), ללא התלות בספריות חיצוניות כבדות.

הנה השינויים:

payload.text: ה + וה - של ההסתרת ילדים לא תמיד עובד בלחיצה ראשונה. לפעמים צריך ללחוץ פעמיים כדי שיפעל

**Assessing Button Functionality**

I've been looking into the expand/collapse button's inconsistent behavior. The current focus is on pinpointing why a single click doesn't always register, sometimes needing a double-click. My analysis leans towards investigating event listeners and their responsiveness in the family tree node structure. I am digging deeper into the specific implementation to see what might be interfering with proper function.


**Pinpointing Click Event Conflicts**

I'm now focusing on the event handling within `family-tree.component.ts`. I see a node-level click listener and a separate listener for the toggle buttons. My key observation is `event.stopPropagation()` in the button's listener. This prevents the button click from triggering the node's click handler. I need to ensure the button click always takes precedence, and that the stop propagation is not the root of my problem.


**Analyzing Drag Interaction**

I've realized the D3 drag behavior attached to each node is likely interfering with the button clicks. The drag handler intercepts mouse events, and even slight movements are interpreted as a drag, suppressing click events. The priority is to confirm this, and understand how the drag handler is interfering with the button's event listeners, potentially causing intermittent function.


**Prioritizing Event Handling**

I've been examining the `d3.drag()` implementation, focusing on how it impacts the button's `click` event. I've found a "Click event not firing inside draggable element" issue. After some thought, I now feel using a filter on the drag behavior is the cleanest approach. I'm going to modify the D3 drag definition to exclude the toggle button, using the `filter()` method, or looking at the `event.sourceEvent.target` to prevent drag from interrupting the click events.


**Refining Drag Exclusion Logic**

I'm now refining the logic to exclude the toggle button from the drag behavior. Initially, I used `.closest('.toggle-btn')`, but I'm now exploring `event.target` in conjunction with class names. I've realized the toggle button itself is an SVG group with the class `toggle-btn`. I'm going to look at using the `.filter()` method to target elements *inside* the toggle button to make sure dragging is never triggered.


**Confirming Click Precedence**

I'm now implementing the `filter` method within the D3 drag behavior to exclusively disable drag when clicking the toggle button. I'm focusing on ensuring click events on the button consistently trigger the intended actions without any interruption from the drag interaction. The `closest` method on `event.target` should work fine to determine the toggle button's presence, so now it's about making it bulletproof. I feel I am closing in on a clean solution.


**Verifying Event Exclusion**

I've implemented the `filter` method on the D3 drag behavior. I'm focusing on ensuring the button is completely excluded from drag events. I'm now testing the code, specifically checking how the button functions under all conditions. I'm verifying the button click is always prioritized and that dragging only works when initiated outside the button itself.


**Testing Drag Behavior Exclusion**

I am now doing some final testing, making sure that there are no remaining inconsistencies with the button's click behavior. I'm focusing specifically on verifying that the D3 drag behavior completely ignores clicks *within* the button's area. With the new filter in place, I expect the button to behave as a standard HTML element with reliable click response.


**Validating XML Structure**

I'm now generating the XML output, ensuring the `<change>` structure aligns with the intended format, including the file path, description, and content placeholders. After thinking more, I am looking at the node click event handler within the `nodeEnter.on('click', ...)` callback. I'm verifying the `event.stopPropagation()` on the button, and confirming this will now work as intended, since the D3 drag filter takes precedence.


**Writing Filter Implementation**

I'm now writing the `d3.drag().filter()` implementation. I'm leveraging the raw DOM event provided in D3 v7+ to ensure that clicks within the toggle button's area are completely ignored by the drag behavior. I am focusing on making sure that the button click handler works every time.

הבעיה שתיארת נובעת מהתנגשות בין התנהגות הגרירה (Drag) של ה-D3 לבין אירוע הלחיצה (Click) של הכפתור. כאשר לוחצים על הכפתור, הספרייה לעיתים מפרשת את הלחיצה כהתחלה של גרירה (אפילו אם העכבר זז פיקסל אחד), ולכן "בולעת" את אירוע הלחיצה בפעם הראשונה.

הפתרון הוא להגדיר ל-`d3.drag` להתעלם מאירועי גרירה שמתחילים על הכפתור (`toggle-btn`).

הנה התיקון לקובץ ה-TypeScript:

payload.text: למה במצב של שמציג את "כל המשפחה" ל"עשו כהן" אין את ה - כדי להסתיר את הילד ?

**Investigating Toggle Logic**

I'm currently digging into the `FamilyTreeComponent` to understand why Esau Cohen lacks the collapse/expand button in "All Family" view, even though he has children listed in `mock-data.ts`. I'm focused on the button rendering conditions within the component's logic.


**Examining Button Conditions**

I've pinpointed the toggle button's rendering condition within `FamilyTreeComponent`. The code checks if a node has children related to the current member. Specifically, line 282 shows this via `hasChildren`, which is dependent on `allChildNodes` and the `otherParentId` field in `mock-data.ts`.


**Analyzing Parent Relationships**

I've realized the problem lies in how child relationships are established. "Eliphaz" lists `otherParentId: 's5'`, but no matching spouse ('s5') exists for Esau. This breaks the `hasChildren` check. The logic in `enrichData` specifically looks for a `spouseParent` based on `otherParentId` but fails if there's no spouse defined, causing the button to be absent, and `hasChildren` to return `false`.


**Refining Node Relationships**

I'm now revisiting the node structure to understand how Esau, as a child of Yitzhak, is represented in the D3 tree. I'm focusing on why Esau's subtree, particularly Eliphaz, lacks the toggle button. I'm using the `nodeEnter` loop and `hasChildren` function, specifically looking at the condition where `childData.otherParentId === member.id`. I see Eliphaz's `otherParentId` points to 's5', which is problematic since Esau doesn't have a defined spouse with that ID. This mismatch inhibits Esau's button rendering.


**Addressing Data Inconsistencies**

I'm making a critical adjustment to `mock-data.ts`. The toggle button issue stems from a data inconsistency related to Esau's child, Eliphaz. To resolve this, I'm removing the `otherParentId: 's5'` from Eliphaz's entry. This action ensures that the `hasChildren` check, and therefore button rendering, will function as expected. This will fix the bug, as requested.


**Resolving Spouse Identifier**

I've decided to address the data inconsistency by removing the erroneous `otherParentId: 's5'` from Eliphaz. I was going to add a spouse with the matching ID, but the code relies on the absence of `otherParentId` for the standard parent-child relationship. This will resolve the bug, as requested. The more I reviewed the code, the clearer it was that the original design assumes the first child to belong to the main parent if no `otherParentId` is specified.


**Updating Data Inconsistencies**

I'm now identifying the root cause within `mock-data.ts`. The recent "fix" within the data was only a temporary workaround, not a sustainable solution. I've realized the problem lies in the `otherParentId: 's5'` entry, which is absent for the identified spouse ('s5'). I'm focusing on rectifying this data error. I'm removing the reference, as an ID s5 does not exist.


**Refining Data Integrity**

I'm now zeroing in on the problematic data in `mock-data.ts`. I see another child, Kedar Cohen, whose other parent, spouse ID `s6`, is properly defined. So only Esau's child has an issue. I've updated the file, removing the erroneous `otherParentId: 's5'` from Eliphaz's entry. This resolves the bug and satisfies the request.


**Confirming Button Logic**

I'm now confirming the logic within `FamilyTreeComponent`. Removing `otherParentId` from child '7' makes the condition `!childData.otherParentId && index === 0` true for Esau. This is the desired behavior, as it correctly identifies children without a specific spouse. I've double-checked the data and button conditions, and it looks like I've got this fixed. The extracted CSS is still available.


**Analyzing Filter Logic**

I'm now investigating how the `filterMalesOnly` logic interacts with the corrected data and button rendering. The code checks for `otherParentId` and if the spouse is *not* in the list, resets `otherParentId` to `undefined`. This should now function correctly, given my recent `mock-data.ts` update. The component's current functionality looks sound.


**Confirming Mock Data Adjustment**

I've just finished the fix. I've re-reviewed `mock-data.ts`, double-checking the deletion of the problematic `otherParentId: 's5'` from Eliphaz's entry. I'm now certain this will resolve the data inconsistency and enable the collapse/expand button for Esau as expected. This data adjustment directly satisfies the bug fix request.

הסיבה לכך היא שבנתונים (Mock Data), הילד "אליפז כהן" (הבן של עשו) משויך ל"הורה אחר" עם מזהה `s5`, אך ההורה הזה אינו קיים ברשימת בני הזוג של עשו.

במצב "רק גברים", הקוד מנקה אוטומטית שיוכים להורים לא קיימים (כי הנשים מסוננות החוצה), ולכן הכפתור מופיע. במצב "כל המשפחה", השגיאה בנתונים מונעת מהכפתור להופיע כי המערכת מחפשת את ההורה `s5`.

אני מתקן את קובץ הנתונים `src/data/mock-data.ts` ומסיר את השיוך השגוי (`otherParentId: 's5'`) מאליפז, כך שהוא ישויך ישירות לעשו והכפתור יופיע.

תיקנתי את בעיית הלחיצה הכפולה.

**הסבר טכני:**
הבעיה נבעה מכך שספריית D3 זיהתה תזוזות מזעריות של העכבר (שקורות כמעט בכל לחיצה טבעית) כ"גרירה" (Drag), ולכן חסמה את אירוע ה"לחיצה" (Click) כדי למנוע כפילויות.

**מה עשיתי:**
1.  **איחוד לוגיקה:** ביטלתי את אירוע ה-`click` הנפרד והעברתי את בדיקת הלחיצה לתוך סיום הגרירה (`drag.end`).
2.  **סף רגישות (Threshold):** הוספתי בדיקה שמודדת כמה פיקסלים העכבר זז בפועל. אם התזוזה קטנה מ-3 פיקסלים, המערכת תתייחס לפעולה כ"לחיצה" ותפתח את כרטיס המידע. אם התזוזה גדולה יותר, המערכת תתייחס לזה כגרירה של הכרטיס.

עכשיו הלחיצה צריכה להרגיש הרבה יותר חלקה ומיידית.

entries.description: Refactor interaction logic: Combine drag and click handling to fix 'double click' issue caused by micro-movements being interpreted as drags. Implements a threshold-based click detection within the drag end event.
